perturbation.original_text: 2.0 For purposes of this Agreeme nt, “Confidential Information” means any information of a confidential and/or proprietary nature first disclosed to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party in connection with discussions between the parties regarding the Project on, before or after the Effectiv e Date, which information is either: ( a) in documents or other tangible materials clearly marked CONFIDENTIAL or the like at the time of disclosure or otherwise communicated to be confidential; or (b) provided orally or in any other intangible form, if at the time of first disclosure the Disclosing Party tells the Recipient that the information is confidentially; or (c) although not marked “CONFIDENTIAL” or verbally described to be confidential, Recipient reasonably should know the information is confident ial and not generally known to the public.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.0 For purposes of this Agreement, “Confidential Information” means any information disclosed to the Recipient by the Disclosing Party. Information is only considered 'Confidential' if: (a) it is clearly marked CONFIDENTIAL at the time of disclosure; or (b) is verbally communicated to be confidential at the time of first disclosure. Information is NOT confidential if the Recipient should reasonably know the information is confidential and not generally known to the public.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition includes a 'reasonable person' standard for identifying confidential information even if unmarked or undisclosed. The modified text removes this provision, stating that if something is not marked or disclosed as confidential, it is not 'Confidential Information.' This conflicts with the reasonable person standard commonly applied in trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 - Definition of trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law defines trade secrets based partially on whether efforts are made to maintain secrecy that is objectively reasonable under the circumstances. By explicitly stating that information not marked or verbally described as confidential is *not* confidential, the modification weakens trade secret protection if reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality were otherwise made.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.61 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.61 | Uniform trade secrets act definitions. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF As used in sections 1333.61 to 1333.69 of the Revised Code, unless the context requires otherwise: (A) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts Ohio trade secret law by removing the 'reasonable person' standard for determining confidentiality. The original text considers information confidential if a recipient 'reasonably should know the information is confidential', while the changed text explicitly states information is 'NOT confidential' if not marked or verbally disclosed. This directly conflicts with the "reason to know" standard described in Ohio Revised Code Section 1333.61(B)(1), where misappropriation includes acquiring a trade secret when a person 'knows or has reason to know' it was acquired improperly.

perturbation.original_text: 4.0 The Recipient shall not disclose to any other person, firm or corporation, other than its employees, directors, agents, or representatives, including without limitation, attorneys, accountants , appraisers and financial advisors, who have a “need to know” for purposes of completing any assessment of the Project (collectively, “representatives”) any of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party hereunder, by using the same degree of care to avoid disclosure of such Confidential Information as Recipient employs with respect to its own proprieta ry and confidential information of like kind.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.0 The Recipient shall not disclose to any other person, firm or corporation, other than its employees, directors, agents, or representatives, including without limitation, attorneys, accountants , appraisers and financial advisors, who have a “need to know” for purposes of completing any assessment of the Project (collectively, “representatives”) any of the Confidential Information received from the Disclosing Party hereunder, using any degree of care they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Recipient to use 'the same degree of care' as they would use to protect their own proprietary information. The modification allows them to use 'any degree of care they deem appropriate,' potentially lowering the standard of care and weakening the protection of confidential information. This could be problematic under trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.61 - Misappropriation of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law regarding trade secrets hinges on reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By allowing the Recipient to use any level of care, even a low level, the modified text contradicts the requirement that reasonable efforts be made to maintain the secrecy of the information.

(B) "Misappropriation" means any of the following: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without the express or implied consent of the other person by a person who did any of the following: (a) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (b) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret that the person acquired was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (c) Before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (C) "Person" has the same meaning as in division (C) of section 1.59 of the Revised Code and includes governmental entities. (D) "Trade
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Ohio trade secret law. The original contract mandated the Recipient to protect confidential information with 'the same degree of care' as their own proprietary information. The changed text allows the Recipient to use 'any degree of care they deem appropriate,' potentially lowering the standard significantly. According to the Law Explanation, Ohio law hinges on 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By permitting a potentially low level of care, the modified text weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, creating a risk of misappropriation and contradicting the reasonable efforts standard required to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 9.0 The parties agree that irreparable harm may result if Vendor fails to abide by the confidentiality requirements set forth in the ag reement. In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall have the right to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief, without having to prove actual damages or to post a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.0 The parties agree that significant harm may result if Vendor fails to abide by the confidentiality requirements set forth in the agreement. In the event of a breach by Vendor of the confidentiality requirements of this Agreement, the University shall have the right to seek injunctive and/or other preliminary or equitable relief only after proving actual damages.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows the University to seek injunctive relief without proving actual damages. The modification requires proof of actual damages before seeking injunctive relief. This contradicts the typical standard for injunctive relief in trade secret cases.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.63 - Injunctive relief for misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio law allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets, and proving actual damages is typically *not* a prerequisite for obtaining an injunction, especially in cases where the harm is difficult to quantify. This change makes it more difficult for the University to protect its confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.63 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.63 | Damages recoverable. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: July 20, 1994 Latest Legislation: House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant in a civil action is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages may include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty that is equitable under the circumstances considering the loss to the complainant, the benefit to the misappropriator, or both, for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (B) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award punitive or exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding three times any award made under division (A) of this section. Available Versions of this Section July 20, 1994 – House Bill 320 - 120th General Assembly [ View July 20, 1994 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text requires proving actual damages before the University can seek injunctive relief, which contradicts the typical standard for trade secret cases, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses damages recoverable for misappropriation under Section 1333.63 of the Ohio Revised Code, it does not explicitly state that proving actual damages is a prerequisite for injunctive relief. However, the snippet does emphasize damages recoverable for misappropriation, which implies that remedies beyond damages may be available and are not necessarily contingent upon proving actual damages. The original text allows injunctive relief without proving actual damages; the modified text directly contradicts this, weakening the University's ability to protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: 10.0 This Agreement and any performance hereunder shall be interpreted in accordance with and shall be governed by the laws of the State of Ohio, wi thout giving effect to the principles of conflict of laws thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.0 This Agreement and any performance hereunder shall be interpreted in accordance with and shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, wi thout giving effect to the principles of conflict of laws thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Ohio law governs the agreement. The modification changes this to Delaware law. While parties can choose governing law, if the University (Dayton, OH) suffers harm due to actions in Ohio, asserting Delaware law may be challenged, especially if it undermines Ohio's protections for trade secrets or confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of law principles and potential conflicts with Ohio's interest in protecting businesses within its borders.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law in a contract, courts may refuse to honor that choice if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the litigation (here, Ohio protecting businesses within the state). If Delaware law offered significantly less protection for trade secrets, an Ohio court might disregard the choice of law provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Ohio to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that courts might refuse to honor a choice of law if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest in the litigation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that if the University (Dayton, OH) suffers harm due to actions in Ohio, asserting Delaware law might be challenged, especially if it undermines Ohio's protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. Here, the choice of Delaware law could contradict Ohio's interest in protecting its businesses, creating a legal contradiction because the modified clause could fail legal scrutiny if Delaware law provides less protection.
